title: Zero Trust Architecture Lab : Adversarial Validation of a Dual-Site Homelab Security Architecture
summary: Full walkthrough of architecting a dual-device Zero Trust lab aligned with NIST SP 800-207, using pfSense, Suricata, Authelia, Traefik, Splunk, and Cloudflare Zero Trust.
content:
# Zero Trust Architecture Lab — Engineering Case Study

**Karim Abdel-Nasser Rady**
---

## Overview

This document describes the design, implementation, and operational engineering of ZTA Lab — a dual-site Zero Trust Architecture lab built across two physical hosts and 8 virtual machines.

The project was built to study Zero Trust principles in a hands-on, multi-site environment: how access control is enforced across multiple independent layers, how telemetry is collected and normalized from heterogeneous sources, how log pipelines are designed for signal quality rather than volume, and how detection logic is structured to produce meaningful visibility rather than alert noise.

The architecture is guided by **NIST SP 800-207**. No component is trusted by default. Every access request is authenticated, authorized, and logged regardless of where it originates on the network.

---

## Design Goals

Before any component was deployed, a set of design questions shaped the architecture:

- How do you enforce identity verification at every access point without relying on network location as a trust signal?
- How do you build a log pipeline that delivers high-fidelity, enriched events without exhausting ingestion limits?
- How do you segment a multi-site environment so that a compromised host in one zone cannot directly reach management infrastructure in another?
- How do you validate that the monitoring pipeline actually captures events without introducing noise that obscures real signal?

The answers to these questions determined the component selection, network topology, log routing design, and detection logic described in this document.

---

## Threat Model and Scope

Defining the trust boundary explicitly is what separates engineered architecture from assembled tooling.

| Assumption | Detail |
|---|---|
| No implicit trust based on network position | A host inside the network receives no inherent authorization |
| Identity must be verified per session | TOTP required on every new access session |
| Contextual signals inform access decisions | Geographic origin and device posture are evaluated per request |
| Cloudflare control plane is assumed uncompromised | Edge enforcement is treated as a trusted policy boundary |
| Physical host compromise is out of scope | Hypervisor and Windows 11 host security are not modeled |
| East-west movement between zones is restricted by default | Cross-VLAN traffic requires explicit firewall rules |

**Out of scope for this version:**

- Active Directory and Windows-based authentication chains
- Compromise of the Cloudflare account or tunnel token
- Supply chain attacks on Docker images or pfSense packages
- Full SOAR-based automated response

---

## Architecture

### Physical Infrastructure

The lab spans two physical Windows 11 machines running VirtualBox, connected over a home network. Each machine hosts a pfSense 2.7 firewall with Suricata IDS and a set of VMs in isolated VLANs. An IKEv2 IPsec tunnel bridges the two sites, carrying log data and application traffic.

| Device | Role | RAM |
|---|---|---|
| Device 1 (D1) | Security gateway, SIEM host, ZTA enforcement | 16 GB |
| Device 2 (D2) | User network, application servers, log aggregation | 16 GB |

### VM Inventory

| VM | Device | VLAN | IP | Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| pfSense-D1 | D1 | Trunk | 192.168.10.1 / 192.168.20.1 | Firewall, IPsec, Suricata |
| siem-vm | D1 | 10 | 192.168.10.20 | Splunk SIEM, Vector D1 |
| services-vm | D1 | 20 | 192.168.20.10 | Traefik, Authelia, cloudflared |
| pfSense-D2 | D2 | Trunk | 192.168.30.1 / 192.168.40.1 | Firewall, IPsec, Suricata |
| appserver-vm | D2 | 30 | 192.168.30.50 | DVWA application server, auditd |
| user-vm-1 | D2 | 30 | 192.168.30.10 | Simulated user workstation |
| user-vm-2 | D2 | 30 | 192.168.30.20 | Simulated user workstation |
| collector-vm | D2 | 30 | 192.168.30.100 | Vector D2 log aggregator |
| kali-vm | D2 | 40 | 192.168.40.10 | Isolated test client |

### Network Zones and Trust Model

| Zone | VLAN | Trust Level | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| MGMT | 10 | Restricted | Splunk SIEM and management infrastructure |
| SERVICES | 20 | Controlled | ZTA enforcement point (Traefik + Authelia) |
| USERS | 30 | Semi-trusted | User workstations, app server, log collector |
| ISOLATED | 40 | Untrusted | Isolated zone for traffic generation and testing |

The firewall policy between zones directly reflects the trust model:

| Source | Destination | Action | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| VLAN 10 | Any | Allow | Management has full network reach |
| VLAN 20 | VLAN 10 | Allow | Traefik routes to Splunk backend |
| VLAN 20 | Internet | Allow | cloudflared outbound tunnel |
| VLAN 30 | VLAN 10 | Allow | Log forwarding via HEC over IPsec |
| VLAN 30 | Internet | Allow | General application access |
| VLAN 40 | VLAN 10 | **Block** | Isolated zone cannot reach SIEM |
| VLAN 40 | VLAN 20 | **Block** | Isolated zone cannot reach ZTA proxy |
| VLAN 40 | VLAN 30 | Allow | Controlled access to user zone for testing |

> VLAN segmentation alone is not Zero Trust. These rules enforce zone boundaries but do not replace identity verification, MFA, or continuous monitoring — all of which are implemented independently.

![High level.png](images/1778532890129-high-level.png)

---

## Zero Trust Access Control

### Design Philosophy

The access control model applies three independent enforcement layers before any request reaches a backend. A failure at any layer terminates the request. No single bypass path exists because each layer operates independently of the others.

```
External Browser
  │
  ▼
[1] Cloudflare Edge
    - Context-aware access policy (identity + country + device posture)
    - Custom WAF rules (offensive tool User-Agent blocking)
    - Geographic restriction expression
    - Rate limiting scoped per hostname
  │
  ▼ (policy pass only)
[2] cloudflared Tunnel
    - Outbound-only connection from services-vm to Cloudflare
    - No inbound firewall ports required on Device 1
    - Traffic proxied through Cloudflare, never directly to the network
  │
  ▼
[3] Traefik Reverse Proxy (services-vm)
    - forwardAuth middleware on every declared route
    - Delegates authentication to Authelia before proxying
    - File-based route provider for stability
  │
  ▼ (forwardAuth pass only)
[4] Authelia MFA
    - TOTP required per session
    - Account lockout after 3 failed attempts within 2 minutes
    - 30-minute inactivity timeout · 8-hour maximum session
    - default_policy: deny — undeclared routes are blocked
  │
  ▼ (MFA pass only)
[5] Backend Application
    - Splunk, DVWA, or Traefik dashboard
    - Never directly reachable outside this chain
```

![Authentication & Access Flow.jpeg](images/1778532909640-authentication---access-flow.jpeg)

### Layer 1 — Cloudflare Edge

Cloudflare serves as the outermost policy enforcement point. All external access is routed through Cloudflare's network via a `cloudflared` tunnel — no direct inbound connections are accepted on Device 1.

Three independent checks are evaluated per request:

**Identity check** — An email-based allowlist is enforced through Cloudflare Access. Non-permitted identities are rejected at the edge before reaching the tunnel.

**Geographic check** — A custom WAF expression blocks requests originating outside the permitted country. This is an explicit expression written for this deployment, not an automatic Cloudflare feature.

**Device posture check** — WARP client enrollment is required. Unenrolled devices are rejected regardless of credentials or geographic origin.

Additional WAF configuration:
- Custom rules block known offensive tool User-Agents (`sqlmap`, `nikto`, `hydra`, `gobuster`, `masscan`)
- Rate limiting blocks endpoints receiving more than 30 requests in 10 seconds, with a 1-minute ban
- All rules scoped to specific hostnames to avoid matching internal service traffic

This is context-aware access enforcement — combining identity, geographic origin, and device posture per request. It is not full enterprise ABAC, which typically incorporates dynamic risk scoring and role metadata, but it applies multiple independent contextual signals to every request.

### Layer 2 — Traefik (Policy Enforcement Point)

Traefik v2.11 acts as the Policy Enforcement Point between the Cloudflare tunnel and backend applications. Every declared route uses `forwardAuth` middleware, which passes the request to Authelia for verification before proxying.

After Authelia approves a request, Traefik forwards verified identity headers (`Remote-User`, `Remote-Groups`, `Remote-Name`, `Remote-Email`) to the backend. Applications receive session identity without managing authentication themselves.

Route definitions use the file-based provider (`--providers.file.directory`) rather than the Docker socket provider, which proved unstable in this deployment.

### Layer 3 — Authelia (Identity and Session Control)

Authelia enforces multi-factor authentication and manages session lifecycle. Configuration uses `default_policy: deny` — any route not explicitly declared in the access control rules is blocked without an allow decision.

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| MFA method | TOTP (time-based one-time password) |
| Session expiration | 8 hours |
| Inactivity timeout | 30 minutes |
| Failed attempt lockout | 3 attempts within 2 minutes |
| Ban duration | 30 minutes |
| Remember me | Disabled |

### Legitimate User Access Lifecycle

A user connecting to a protected application from outside the network follows this exact flow:

1. Browser navigates to `splunk.<domain>` — Cloudflare evaluates identity, country, and device posture
2. On policy pass, Cloudflare proxies the request through the cloudflared tunnel to Traefik on services-vm
3. Traefik evaluates the route — `forwardAuth` sends an authentication check to Authelia
4. If no valid session exists, Authelia redirects the user to the login portal at `auth.<domain>`
5. User enters credentials — Authelia verifies against the user database
6. User provides TOTP code — Authelia validates and creates a session
7. Authelia redirects back to the original URL with an authorization response header
8. Traefik receives the `forwardAuth` approval, attaches identity headers, and proxies the request to the Splunk backend at `192.168.10.20:8000`
9. Session persists for up to 8 hours with a 30-minute inactivity check

No step in this flow can be bypassed from outside the chain. The application backend on siem-vm is not directly reachable — it is only accessible via the Traefik route with a valid Authelia session.

---

## Telemetry and Log Engineering

### Log Sources

The monitoring pipeline ingests events from eight distinct sources across both sites:

| Source | VM | Protocol | Port | Log Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Linux syslog (auth, sudo) | user-vm-1, user-vm-2, appserver-vm | rsyslog UDP | 514 | System auth events |
| auditd (syscall events) | appserver-vm | rsyslog UDP | 514 | Command execution, file access |
| pfSense filterlog | pfSense-D1, pfSense-D2 | Syslog UDP | 515 | Firewall pass/block events |
| Suricata EVE JSON | pfSense-D1, pfSense-D2 | Syslog UDP | 516 | IDS alerts |
| Journald (D1 services) | siem-vm, services-vm | Vector journald | local | Service and container events |
| Docker container logs | services-vm | Vector Docker | local | Traefik, Authelia, cloudflared |
| Authelia audit log | services-vm | Vector file | local | Authentication events |
| Traefik access log | services-vm | Vector file | local | HTTP request logs |

### Pipeline Architecture

Log data flows through two Vector instances before reaching Splunk:

```
D2 Sources (VLAN 30)
  user-vm-1        ──► rsyslog :514 ──┐
  user-vm-2        ──► rsyslog :514 ──┤
  appserver-vm     ──► rsyslog :514 ──┼──► collector-vm (Vector D2) ──► Splunk HEC :8088
  pfSense-D2       ──► syslog  :515 ──┤         (filter · parse · enrich)    (via IPsec)
  Suricata-D2      ──► syslog  :516 ──┘

D1 Sources (VLAN 10/20)
  siem-vm          ──► journald        ──┐
  services-vm      ──► syslog  :514   ──┤
  pfSense-D1       ──► syslog  :515   ──┼──► siem-vm (Vector D1) ──► Splunk HEC :8088
  Suricata-D1      ──► syslog  :516   ──┤         (filter · enrich)       (localhost)
  Authelia log     ──► file            ──┤
  Traefik log      ──► file            ──┘
```

The two-instance design is intentional. A single collector on D2 would require management traffic (log data) to traverse an untrusted path before encryption. By running Vector locally on each site and transmitting only over the IPsec tunnel, log delivery is encrypted end-to-end and isolated from application traffic.

![Logs Flow Diagram.jpeg](images/1778532932968-logs-flow-diagram.jpeg)

### Noise Reduction Strategy

Splunk Free limits ingestion to 500 MB/day. Without filtering, six VMs running normal system activity would saturate that limit before a single security-relevant event is collected. The first stage of every Vector pipeline is a noise filter that drops high-volume, low-value events before they reach the enrichment layer.

| Category | Source | Reason Filtered |
|---|---|---|
| CRON job execution | All VMs | Scheduled tasks produce no security signal |
| apt / dpkg activity | All VMs | Package manager events are routine maintenance noise |
| NTP sync (systemd-timesyncd) | All VMs | Clock synchronization has no security value here |
| NetworkManager events | All VMs | Interface events are expected and non-actionable |
| snapd activity | All VMs | Background snap refresh produces no signal |

**Result: ~85% reduction in total log volume** while preserving all authentication, authorization, audit, firewall, and IDS events.

### Field Enrichment and Normalization

After filtering, VRL (Vector Remap Language) transforms parse raw syslog text into structured fields. This normalization step is what makes detection queries precise and maintainable — without it, every rule would require fragile regex against inconsistent raw text.

| Source | Enriched Fields Produced |
|---|---|
| SSH auth events | `auth_event`, `ssh.src_ip`, `ssh.user`, `ssh.port` |
| sudo commands | `sudo.user`, `sudo.run_as`, `sudo.cmd` |
| auditd syscalls | `audit_event` (`cmd_exec`, `net_connect`, `auth`) |
| Suricata EVE JSON | `alert_sig`, `alert_severity`, `alert_category` |
| pfSense filterlog | `firewall.src_ip`, `firewall.dst_ip`, `firewall.action`, `firewall.proto` |
| All events | `source_site`, `lab_zone` (for cross-site correlation) |

The `source_site` and `lab_zone` fields are particularly important in a multi-site environment — they allow a single SPL query to distinguish whether an event originated from VLAN 10 on D1 or VLAN 30 on D2 without requiring separate indexes or source filters.

---

## Access Control Validation

To confirm the architecture matched its intended design, several access paths were tested:

| Attempted Access | Expected Result | Confirmed |
|---|---|---|
| VLAN 40 → siem-vm (192.168.10.20) | Blocked by firewall | Request timed out |
| VLAN 40 → services-vm (192.168.20.10) | Blocked by firewall | Request timed out |
| External access without WARP enrollment | Rejected by Cloudflare | Cloudflare 403 |
| External access outside permitted country | Blocked by WAF geo rule | Cloudflare 403 |
| Authelia login without valid TOTP | Session rejected | Login refused at MFA step |
| Route with no Authelia policy | Blocked by `default_policy: deny` | Traefik 403 |

These confirmations validated that the architecture behaved as designed — not as a claim of adversarial resilience, but as a functional check that each control was active and correctly configured.

---

## NIST SP 800-207 Principles Applied

| Principle | Implementation |
|---|---|
| No implicit trust | All externally initiated requests pass through Cloudflare, Traefik, and Authelia regardless of network source |
| All communication secured | HTTPS via Traefik TLS · AES-256-GCM IPsec for inter-site traffic · outbound-only cloudflared tunnel |
| Access granted per session | TOTP required per session · 8-hour maximum · 30-minute inactivity timeout |
| Dynamic policy enforcement | Identity, geographic origin, and device posture evaluated per request at the Cloudflare layer |
| Continuous monitoring | Vector + Splunk provide real-time enriched event collection across all zones |
| Least privilege | Firewall rules default to deny between zones — access requires explicit policy |

---

## Operational Engineering Challenges

These are issues encountered during deployment, included because they reflect real infrastructure behavior rather than ideal documentation.

**Cloudflare WAF self-rate-limiting** — A rate limiting rule using `http.host contains "<domain>"` also matched Splunk's internal API calls (~50 requests per page load), causing the dashboard to trigger its own rate limit. Fixed by scoping rules to specific application hostnames.

**Vector startup race condition** — Vector initializing before Splunk's HEC endpoint was ready produced sustained `Healthcheck timed out` errors. Fixed with a startup wait loop polling HEC health before starting Vector.

**rsyslog silent failure on fresh Ubuntu installs** — The `imjournal` module fails when `/var/lib/rsyslog/` does not exist. `systemctl status rsyslog` reports active regardless of whether log forwarding is actually working. This directory must be created before deploying rsyslog on any fresh Ubuntu install — the failure is completely silent.

**Vector VRL syntax constraints** — `.field ?? ""` throws `unnecessary error coalescing operation` in Vector 0.55 because field access returns null, not an error. Correct form: `string(.field) ?? ""`. Multi-line negation expressions also fail at parse time — all filter conditions must be expressed on a single line.

**Suricata EVE JSON forwarding reset** — Suricata resets its EVE JSON syslog destination after every service restart or pfSense GUI update. After any restart, the remote syslog server and port must be manually re-entered in the WebGUI. This caused silent alert forwarding failures several times during testing.

**IPsec validation method** — pfSense's built-in diagnostic ping uses locally generated traffic that bypasses IPsec routing, making it unsuitable for tunnel connectivity validation. VM-to-VM ping tests are required to confirm that the IPsec tunnel is carrying actual traffic.

**Traefik Docker provider instability** — The Docker socket provider intermittently produced routes that appeared configured in the dashboard but served no traffic after container restarts. File-based provider with `--providers.file.directory` proved reliable throughout the lab's lifecycle.

**Authelia config file corruption** — In certain terminal environments, writing YAML via heredoc converts `server.host` into a hyperlink, silently corrupting the file. All configuration files were written using Python's `open().write()` to avoid this.

---

## Known Limitations

| Limitation | Impact |
|---|---|
| No EDR/XDR platform | Endpoint visibility relies on auditd syscall rules and rsyslog |
| Linux-only environment | No Windows attack surface — Active Directory, NTLM, and Kerberos are untested |
| No Active Directory | Authentication is local-account and SSH-key based only |
| No SOAR automation | Alert response is manual — detection fires but no automated containment |
| Splunk Free ingestion cap | 500 MB/day constrains log retention and high-volume scenarios |
| No hardware TAP or SPAN | Network visibility is IDS-based only — no full packet capture |
| Detection rules are prototypes | SPL rules have not been tuned against production-volume log data |

---

## Key Metrics

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Physical machines | 2 |
| Virtual machines | 8 |
| VLANs / trust zones | 4 |
| Log sources integrated | 8 |
| Enriched fields produced by VRL | 15+ |
| Log volume reduction via noise filtering | ~85% |
| ZTA enforcement layers | 3 independent (Cloudflare · Traefik · Authelia) |
| Cross-site log delivery | Splunk HEC over IPsec tunnel |
| MITRE ATT&CK techniques with telemetry coverage | 14 |

---

## What's Next

**Near-term**
- [ ] Splunk Universal Forwarder on services-vm for Docker container log visibility
- [ ] Wazuh FIM integration across all VMs for file integrity monitoring
- [ ] Automated abuse.ch C2 blocklist refresh into threat intel lookup

**Medium-term**
- [ ] Windows Server VM for Active Directory scenarios and Windows event log ingestion
- [ ] Expand SPL rule library with MITRE ATT&CK coverage heatmap
- [ ] Structured access policy documentation per application in Authelia

**Long-term**
- [ ] Replace Splunk Free with Elastic to remove the ingestion cap
- [ ] SOAR integration (n8n or Shuffle) for automated alert response
- [ ] Formal detection rule tuning methodology

---

## Conclusions

This project demonstrates practical implementation across five engineering domains that are often treated as separate disciplines:

**Network architecture** — Designing a segmented multi-site environment where trust zones are enforced at the firewall layer rather than assumed from network position.

**Identity and access control** — Building a three-layer enforcement chain (Cloudflare context policy → Traefik forwardAuth → Authelia MFA) that applies consistently to every application route regardless of source.

**Telemetry engineering** — Designing a log pipeline that aggregates heterogeneous sources, normalizes them into a consistent field schema, filters noise systematically, and delivers enriched events to a central SIEM across a VPN tunnel.

**Detection design** — Writing SPL rules against structured fields rather than raw text, structured to detect behavioral patterns across multiple event types rather than individual log lines.

**Operational troubleshooting** - Diagnosing infrastructure failures across a multi-component stack where failures are often silent and root causes require cross-layer investigation.

The core architectural conclusion is that Zero Trust is a design commitment across every layer, not a property of any individual component. Cloudflare alone enforces edge policy but has no visibility into internal routing. Authelia alone manages sessions but cannot enforce network segmentation. pfSense alone segments the network but cannot verify identity. The access model only functions as intended when all layers are correctly connected and operating together.

---

*Stack: Splunk 9.3.2 · Vector 0.55.0 · Traefik v2.11 · Authelia · pfSense 2.7 · Suricata · Cloudflare Zero Trust · Docker · Ubuntu 22.04/24.04 · VirtualBox 7.x*

*Zero Trust Architecture Lab - Karim Abdel-Nasser Rady*
